narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 62.8% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This prediction draws from key details in your profile, like your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 22.50, bottom-decile rankings in both your first- and third-year school grades, low family income bracket, Asian racial background, female gender, and plan to attend full-time. Compared to typical applicants—who average around a 3.21 GPA, 36.70 LSAT, mid-level grade deciles near 5.5 to 5.7, and higher income brackets—your metrics stand out as notably lower in several academic areas, which tipped the scales. The strongest influence pushing the model toward predicting failure was your LSAT score, far below the norm and signaling potential challenges in the rigorous reasoning needed for the bar. Next in impact were your third-year grade decile and first-year grade decile, both at the bottom of your class, unlike the average student's middle-of-the-pack performance. Your low family income followed closely, as it often correlates with added barriers seen in past data. Your racial background as Asian, underrepresented compared to the mostly White dataset, also nudged the prediction toward failure, along with your undergraduate GPA. Less influential but still contributing were your gender and full-time enrollment plans, both common but not enough to offset the others. Overall, these factors ranked in that order of influence, with every one pushing the model toward expecting a first-try bar failure rather than success. While disheartening, this isn't set in stone—your strongest paths forward involve retaking the LSAT after targeted prep to boost that critical score closer to or above the 36.70 average. Similarly, building stronger academic habits now could improve future grade deciles if you're still in school or pursuing further credentials. With focused effort on these changeable areas, you could shift your profile toward the successful patterns we've seen in students who passed the bar. We believe in your potential and encourage reapplying after these improvements.
